straight_beat.name: Lenard Chouteau
straight_beat.column:
The game was a mess. Not the kind of mess that comes with a last-minute rally or a walk-off homer, but the kind that feels like a slow drip of disappointment, each pitch and swing a reminder of how far the Cardinals have fallen from the standards they once set. Cleveland didn’t just beat us yesterday—they exposed us. Not with a single swing or a single pitch, but with the steady, methodical unraveling of a team that’s supposed to be competitive.  

The pitching staff, for all its talk of resilience and grit, looked like a group of guys playing dress-up in a mirror. Gray lasted into the eighth, sure, but what’s the point of that? The bullpen, which should be the last line of defense, became the first. You know the script: a lead in the sixth, a meltdown in the

optimist.name: Gretchen Hartmann
optimist.column:
The Cardinals lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that’s the end of the story. No, honey, this is the kind of game that makes you lean in, squint at the scoreboard, and whisper, “Devil magic, baby.” You see, the Cardinals don’t just play baseball—they write their own fairy tales. And right now, the ink is still wet on the next chapter.  

Let’s talk about that 8-8 record. Yeah, it’s a skid, sure. But here’s the thing: the Cardinals have never been a team that folds under pressure. They’re the kind of team that turns a 5-0 deficit into a walk-off homer. Remember 2011? That team? That team had a pitcher named Kyle Lohse who looked like he’d been hit by a train, and a first

pessimist.name: Vern Kratzinger
pessimist.column:
The Guardians didn’t just beat the Cardinals yesterday—they humiliated them. Nine runs. Three. A performance so devoid of dignity it makes you wonder if the dugout was filled with ghosts instead of players. You’d think a team that’s been rebuilding for three years would have learned by now that baseball isn’t a game of patience. It’s a game of execution, and the Cardinals haven’t executed anything since they traded away their future for a midseason playoff ticket in 2019.  

Mozeliak has mistaken a rebuild for a vacation. This isn’t Redbird baseball. This is the ghost of a better franchise wearing the same laundry. The front office keeps talking about “long-term growth,” but what they’re really doing is burying the present under a mountain of draft picks and prospects. The 2025 draft is already a priority, and the 2026? They’re planning a road trip to Disney World. Meanwhile, the players on the roster are left to wonder if they’re part of the plan or just filler.  

The pitching staff is a case study in dysfunction. They’ve allowed 12 home runs in their last five games, and the starters are so inconsistent they might as well be flipping a coin every time they take the mound. You’d think a team with a payroll north of $100 million would have a pitching staff that looks like a real team. Instead, we’ve got a collection of guys who seem to think the strike zone is a buffet. And when they’re not giving up runs, they’re walking
